title: Quantum Otto engine powered by an anisotropic Heisenberg XYZ model under independent local magnetic fields
authors: Meiru Li, Maimaitiyiming Tusun, Fang Zhao, Hasiyatihan Abudoula, Tongcheng Wei
abstract: We study a quantum Otto heat engine whose working substance is an anisotropic two-qubit Heisenberg XYZ model. Independent local magnetic fields are used to control each spin individually. The influence of the longitudinal coupling, anisotropy, transverse coupling, and local fields on the net work output and efficiency is systematically examined. Reducing the longitudinal coupling is found to markedly improve both the maximum work and the peak efficiency. The engine performance reaches an optimum at a particular value of the anisotropy parameter. A local work analysis clarifies how work is produced during the cycle. Because of the asymmetric local fields and the intrinsic spin-spin interaction, the two qubits play markedly different thermodynamic roles; the interaction term itself contributes crucially to the total work. We further analyze the variation of quantum entanglement, quantified by concurrence, along the cycle. The results indicate that a pronounced change in entanglement between the hot and cold isomagnetic strokes is closely correlated with the efficiency enhancement. This work offers new insight into the operating principles and control of quantum Otto heat engines.
summary: The paper analyzes a quantum Otto engine powered by an anisotropic two-qubit Heisenberg XYZ model. It systematically maps out the relationship between coupling strengths, magnetic fields, and energy extraction efficiency. The key finding is that entanglement asymmetry acts as a measurable witness of underlying energy level restructuring responsible for enhancing work output.
main_problem: The study investigates how an anisotropic two-qubit Heisenberg XYZ model can power a quantum Otto heat engine under independent local magnetic fields.
main_result: Engine efficiency is maximized by tuning the anisotropy parameter, and entanglement asymmetry between hot and cold strokes correlates strongly with performance enhancement. The work done is functionally partitioned among the qubits and their interaction term.
method: The authors systematically analyze the quantum Otto cycle using exact diagonalization of the Hamiltonian and calculating thermodynamic quantities via partition functions and local work decomposition.
model_or_system: The system is an anisotropic two-qubit Heisenberg XYZ model, whose dynamics are governed by a Hamiltonian incorporating coupling terms ($J, \Delta, J_z$) and independent local magnetic fields ($B_1, B_2$).
key_observables: Net work output (W_net), efficiency ($\eta$), quantum entanglement (concurrence), and the local work contributions from each qubit and their interaction.
important_parameters: Anisotropy parameter ($\Delta$), transverse coupling ($J$), longitudinal coupling ($J_z$), and the asymmetry of the local magnetic fields ($B_1, B_2$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on treating the cycle using an ideal adiabatic limit for work strokes and assuming specific field control protocols.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the four steps of the Otto cycle; one shows efficiency ($\eta$) vs. $J$ for various $\Delta$, mapping out optimal operating regimes and level crossing regions.
paper_structure: The paper systematically analyzes the Hamiltonian, calculates thermodynamic quantities across the four strokes (adiabatic/isomagnetic), decomposes the work into local contributions ($w_1, w_2, w_{	ext{int}}$), and finally correlates efficiency enhancement with changes in quantum entanglement.
why_interesting: This work provides detailed insights into how microscopic quantum correlations (entanglement) can be linked to macroscopic thermodynamic performance metrics like efficiency in engineered quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Sketch of the quantum Otto cycle.

Figure 2: Efﬁciency η versus transverse coupling J for several values of the anisotropy ∆. Com- mon parameters: Jz = 1.0, Th = 2.0, Tc = 1.0, B1h = 3.0, B2h = 0.0, B1c = 0.8, B2c = 0.8. The black dashed line marks the Carnot limit ηCarnot = 0.5. Shaded gray regions indicate param- eter regimes where no net work can be extracted, i.e., where the heat engine does not operate in a valid engine mode. Dotted segments mark regions with level crossings, where normal heatengine operation is also forbidden.

Figure 3: Energylevel evolution during the linear timedependent variation of the local magnetic ﬁelds, corresponding to the two starmarked points in Fig. 2. The solid lines from bottom to top at the initial time represent E1, E3, E2, and E4, respectively.

Figure 4: Contour plots of net work Wnet and efﬁciency η in the (B1h, B2h) plane for Jz = 0.5. Common parameters: ∆= 1.0, J = 2.0, Th = 2.0, Tc = 1.0, (B1c, B2c) = (0.8, 0.8). The black dashed lines indicate B1h = 0 or B2h = 0.
Figure 5: Same as Fig. 4 but for Jz = 1.0.
